source.quote: human-AI teams produced 50% more ads per worker and higher text quality... human-human teams produced higher image quality, suggesting a jagged frontier of AI agent capability.

requirements.normativeText: Observation: human-AI teams show a jagged productivity frontier — task-specific evaluation is required; do not generalise a single productivity metric.

verification.note: Use-case evaluation runbook cites this finding when scoping metrics.

source.quote: Human-AI teams also produced more homogeneous outputs (or diversity collapse) as their ads were more self-similar.

requirements.normativeText: Observation: diversity collapse is an empirical risk — monitoring should include an output-diversity metric where the business benefits from varied outputs.

implementation.note: Diversity metric not yet applicable to TB review (single deterministic pipeline); relevant to future creative use cases.

verification.note: Add diversity metric when scope expands to generative outputs.

source.quote: human-AI collaboration displayed more delegation, as participants delegated 17% more work to AI agents than to human partners and performed 62% fewer direct text edits when working with AI.

requirements.normativeText: Observation: high delegation rates combined with reduced direct editing correlate with automation-bias risk — oversight effectiveness measures must detect this.

implementation.note: Override-rate and review-duration KPIs serve as proxies.
